speaker: interviewer
text: 20Th of March, Frank Gipps, Carol Wertheim, Sydney, New South Wales, in English. Just say the date again [INAUDIBLE]. 20th of March, 1995. OK, cameras are ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Carol Wertheim, C-A-R-O-L W-E-I-T-H-E-I-M. It's the 20th of March, 1955. And I'm interviewing Frank Gipps at [? Calares, ?] Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, did I say 1995? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And cameras ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Frank Thomas Gipps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-I-P-P-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me your name at birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was spelled the Hungarian way, G-I-P-S-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other names that you're known by?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's the only. That was the Hungarian name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just Frank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11th January, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your age, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well I was-- I was 72 in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your city of birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, could you please describe what your life was like before the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before the occupation of Germany by the-- occupation of Hungary by the Germans, my-- my life was just an average middle-class schoolboy. My father was a barrister. So we had a fairly good education. We learned many languages. We learned-- I learned German when I was six-year-old. I learned French when I was a 12-year-old. And I learned English when I was 15, 16. I learned a bit of Italian also, in between. So that was-- my father considered it is very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Hungary was a very antisemitic country between the wars. And I could already feel the disadvantages of being a Jew, when I came home from the primary school, when I was six, six-and-a-half year old. I came home from the school. And on the way back, as I was going with my little school bag, a couple Gentile-- a couple Christians-- would come up behind me and-- and give a push to my-- my bag ended up in the gutter, and they kicked me in the back. And I-- I turned around, and I said, why-- why did you do that to me? And they said, oh, because you killed Jesus. You killed our God. So that's what you deserve. I says, I didn't kill him. Ah, well, your folk, your people. Obviously, they learned that from their parents. How many children were in your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a brother, who was later on executed by the Hungarian Nazis. And I have a sister, who is still alive. That was the-- our family, so three children, and father, and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what high school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended to the King Matthias High School, which was very near to the royal castle. That's-- that's where I went for eight-- eight long years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your occupation after high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after high school, I couldn't get to university, being Jewish. That was [INAUDIBLE]. So I studied quite a few things. And my-- my favorite to study was acc-- account-- bookkeeping and accounting. And I got a job as a junior accountant. And I still had the job when the Germans marched in, in the 19th of March, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just like to-- sorry-- mention one other thing. During the high school years, you might wish to hear that each time we had a religious class, they-- the Jewish teacher would came, or rabbi would came. And the-- and the headmaster would shout that, 'All stinking Jews out from the class. Your-- your rabbi is here.' So then all the Jews had to get up. So we were about-- there was about 13 Jews from a class of 40 or 50. And then all the Christians would shout, 'Ah, the stinking Jews are going. The air is getting much better now. I wish you would never come back.' And-- and you know, they were throwing missiles at us and kicking us in the back. And so we-- we moved out and we went to the religious-- religious classes. This was just a background atmosphere. But anyhow, it was a Sunday, 19th of March, 1944. It was a beautiful spring weather. I went with some of my friends to a Beethoven concert. And it was-- life was wonderful. Already we knew that war can't go on much longer. The Russians were advancing. They were near the Hungarian border. And that was exactly the reason why Hitler ordered our head of state to let the German army come in, because the Hungarian army couldn't-- obviously couldn't hold back the Russians. So the Germans came in to take up the positions on the border. Well-- well, at that time I had to do part-time labor service. All the Jewish young men had to do, irrespective of age. From 15 to 20, we had to-- after the bombing attacks, when the English and American bombs, we were called out immediately. We had to come at 3 o'clock in the morning, or 5 o'clock in the morning, help with the long hoses to put out the fires. Because if 500-- 500 bombers come, they cause a fair bit of damage. And all the big factories were burning. And the oil refineries were burning. But that was just part time, only after bombing attacks, which happened three, four times a week. And then, of course, all the usual laws come out-- came out. You have to put on your yellow star. Anybody who is caught with the-- with the Christians, especial-- especially sexual relationship, that's immediately heavy-- heavy jail for that, if you are mixing with a non-Jew. And so that-- that was only three or four weeks went past. And then everybody in my-- in my year was called up for full-time forced labor-- I mean, labor-- labor camp. Some of the labor camp work was in Hungary. And lot of others were sent out, of course, to Russia, to-- mostly to pick-- collect mines, minefields.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you sent, Frank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very lucky. I-- I was not sent to Russia, in the first few weeks, anyhow. I was sent to a place near-- near Budapest, only about 100 kilometers away, where we had to get up very early in the morning. We had to walk about 15 kilometers to our workplace, with our rucksack-- backpack in-- in English. And there we had to work mostly cutting out trees, putting trees on tracks and-- oh, physical labor, all different types of physical labor. That's what it was. And we walked back in the evenings. It was hard work, but the-- but the food was not too bad. That was only a very short period that this lasted. And after about four weeks doing this, we-- the order came that we have-- our group has been-- has been ordered to go by train to the border, to the Hungarian-Russian border, to Transylvania, up to the high Carpathian Mountains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all had together-- together, we had a bit of bread left, maybe a bit of saus-- you know, Hungarian-- like [HUNGARIAN] type sausages. And that was enough for about two days. And we took the train. We went 12 hours to the east. And there we had to change trains. We had-- our train didn't go any further. We had to pick up another train, which would have taken us up to the high Carpathian Mountains, to a position-- we knew where we were going. It was a position about 50 miles from the front line, where the Russians were pressing their attacks against the Hungarian army. So we got off the train. And we were inquiring which-- which track is the other train. And at that stage, we were standing out there, about 200 of us, all 20, 21 year old, when two tall men in leather jackets came to our Hungarian commander. And he said-- we were just guessing what he said, but he must have said, where are these Jews going? What's up? Where are you going? Well, the Hungarian said, well, they are doing labor service. They're going to dig trenches against the-- to-- you know, to defend the country against the advancing Russians. And the two officers were German Gestapo officers. And they said to them, well, never mind your train. You just tell your men to follow-- follow us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there were six German behind us, another six in front of us. And they're leading us through the town. The town's name was Nagyvarad-- N-A-G-Y-V-A-R-A-D. It's a big-- fairly big town. We didn't know where we are going. We had no idea what he wants or what it is. But we walked through the town, and eventually we got to the walls of the ghetto. And we have to go through the ghetto. And then-- then we knew where we are. Then we were in the Nagyvarad ghetto. There was 32,000 people inside, pressed together in a couple of streets, about 15 people in each room, and-- well, yeah, about 60, 70 in each flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were inside the ghetto, and that was that. And then all-- all sorts of rumors went around in the ghetto. The rumors-- they ranged from the-- the most optimistic was that Germany needs us. They would-- they would probably use us for labor, working in German factories. The older people-- of course, they couldn't do any work. Well, they-- we guessed that they might be put in special concentration camps or something like that. It was all just guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other thing which happened in the ghetto-- the food was very bad. We had-- once a day, we had a small-- say, half a liter of baked bean soup-- you know, bean soup, but not too much beans in it. And the beans were rotten. They were all very old stock. They were all past the use-by date. So they were-- everybody got diarrhea from it. And that was the only food we-- we had every day, the baked bean soup. Now, we didn't have much to do all day. We were just standing around. So I became friendly with one of the Jewish girls. She was 18, just finished her [? HSC. ?] And we spent a day together. And in the evening, she invited me to her family's place. And there, I could see, in one room, about 60 or 80 people, her whole family-- including grandmothers, grandparents, uncles, great-aunts, and cousins and everybody. And they will be sitting around the table. And I took part in their-- what-- they had some food left, some little bread, and other things. And-- and then the old people were discussing-- they were very sad. Most of them were crying. And later on, I found out that the old people, including my parents-- the educated ones-- they-- they could listen to BBC broadcasts. And what this is just-- I didn't know it at that time, but I found out since then that the BBC was broadcasting from end of '43. They knew it that there are gas chambers in-- in-- somewhere in-- they didn't know the name Auschwitz. But in Poland, Jews are being gassed in great numbers. And these old people-- they listened to it. They knew it. They listened to the English language broadcasts, sometimes even Hungarian language broadcasts. And they all knew it, that they are going to die. Well, they were old anyhow, so they said, in a-- philosophically, well, anyhow, we are old. We-- we have to die anyhow, sooner or later. But at least the young ones will live. They-- they assumed that the young ones will be useful for Germany. Because, I mean, many Germans were dead by then. And they needed the workforce of the young people to-- to produce ammunition and spare parts for the German war effort. So they were optimistic. The young ones will live, but we are finished. So that was the general idea. Anyhow, one-- one morning I went over the next street to-- to find my girlfriend. And the whole street was empty. And that morning, at 5 o'clock, the SS-- the Gestapo-- came, and they drove them all to the trains. And it was the whole street, not one single-- the previous night, there were hundreds and thousands of people milling around. It was absolutely empty. And then, well, I never saw this girlfriend, of course, again. I was searching for her in Auschwitz. But probably she went straight to the gas chamber, I think. Anyhow, from then on, they picked up our brigade, 200 of us-- we were all strong young boys. And with the Hungarian Nazi-- you know, [NON-ENGLISH], which is the country police-- they're extremely cruel people, worse than the SS. They was really sadistic. Anyhow, they ordered us to go from flat to flat, from house to house, and collect everything, what the departed people left behind. So we went into these flats. And photo albums were lying all over the floor, and wedding photos, and all sorts of memories and items. Of course, huge amounts of clothing-- well, everything that's in a normal flat. Now the-- the gendarmes-- they ordered us to roll up all the carpets, take it to some trucks. Then we had to take all the sheets and the blankets to some other trucks. Then we were pushed into the bedrooms, and we had to take out all the bed linen and all the leftover clothing, which they-- again, different trucks came from them. They looted-- I mean, they robbed the whole-- every flat, flat by flat. It was our-- we did the job. They-- they was-- they were holding the guns and the machine guns to us. And they said, if anybody pockets one piece of jewelry, he would be shot on the spot. Anything-- watches we have to hand always to the nearest gendarme-- gendarme just a military police-- I mean the country police. And of course, any piece of-- any wedding rings or anything like that, if they found it in our pocket, no trial and shot on the spot. So we made sure we hand it over. And so we went from day after day. Of course, they-- every day, more and more districts were taken to the wagons, to the cattle trucks, to the cattle wagons. So we were-- we had a very busy time. We were the last strong boys. All the other people in the ghetto were either very small children, babies, or grandmothers and elderly men. It was a lot of women, and right up to 95-year-olds. And then we went to kitchens. In the kitchens, we had to-- there was a big barrel where all the honey had to go in, and a other big barrel where all the apricot jam-- we could mix the jams, actually. Apricot jams and strawberry jams could go in the same barrel. And then the sugar and the salt. And they all had their own barrel, which they then carted away, when it was-- everything was emptied. And the crockery and cutlery had to be taken. And they only left the bare-- they took out the light bulbs. And they took everything what-- what could be taken. So they robbed 32,000 people. Well, I mean, there was a fair bit of material left over. And after a few days, then we had to go to the Dreher beer factory. That was a big factory, where they Gestapo had its headquarters. And under the Gestapo were all the Hungarian interrogators and inquisitors. We called them just inquisitors, because they arrested, every day, a few dozen of the leading citizens. They arrested the doctors, solicitors, accountants, bank managers, wholesale merchants-- impor-- anybody who-- who had a bit of wealth, or a middle-class person. They didn't worry about factory workers or very poor Jews, only the ones who had some assets.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they already took their homes. They already took their-- their whole property and everything what was in their homes. But that wasn't enough of the-- for them. They started to beat these people. And they tortured them with the most cruel methods, not seen since-- since the darkest Middle Ages. Bloody Jew, tell us, where did you hide your gold? Where is your diamond? Where-- where do you keep your diamonds? Where do you keep your gold coins? Because they assumed that every single Jew has mountains of gold and diamonds. Well, quite a few had-- had diamonds, because that was only way to save your assets. You know, and the money, it was-- it wasn't-- there was a very strong inflation. And so many Jews had diamonds. And they had-- they had gold. So they-- they knew what they are doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the beating-- well, we were working around there, in that factory. So we were only 20 meters away from where the beatings took place. They put on records all the time. They played country music and jazz music to try to block out the horrible cries. It was terribly painful. They were-- they were putting nails into their-- I mean, into their fingers, and I know the man got beaten in their-- the testicles got beaten with rubber truncheons and the fact is that when we went back from work in the evening-- well, we didn't-- we didn't have to tell these stories. Because all the people-- the man-- the people who were beaten all day-- they were delivered on stretcher at nighttime. And most of them died. And when this-- I know-- I remember this, when-- when all these dying, and dying or dead people, were delivered back to their families, to their wives and children, and all the others stood around them, and a lot of them went home and took poison right away. They-- they killed themselves, because they knew it. If you see a solicitor coming back, beaten to pulp, the other one knows that tomorrow it will be my turn, so they quickly killed themselves-- killed the wife and then they took it together, husband and wife. Well, there were lots and lots of suicides, dead bodies where I was staying, in that sector. And the-- I mean, the Hungarian gendarmes were desperate. Because they knew it, that the Germans will start the next transport in two days, and the other one in three days. So they only had three, four days to make their money. They will never again in their whole life make so much money as they could make then, in these last couple of days. And some-- obviously, some-- some Jews told them where the money is-- under which tree is it dug in. And others-- well, they rather die. But I'm not going to tell them, because they told their grandchildren where it is, and they thought they will have it after the war. And so they just put up with the suffering and luckily, most of them died the same night. They were beaten up so badly, bruised everywhere. Anyhow, that's just a-- I don't want to go on about this. So anyhow, that's how the days went past. And finally, it was our turn. And then, one morning at 5 o'clock, came the Hungarian military police. And oh! [HUNGARIAN]. Oh, bloody Jews! Quick march. And they put us-- no that-- that was actually at nighttime, not in the morning. At nighttime, they put us in the central synagogue. The whole-- the next day's transport, maybe 6,000 people, were jammed into the synagogue so tightly that people had to lie on the-- on the Torah and-- and every possible place. And I was lying between two benches, together with 10 other people. People urinated on the spot. There was no-- no toilets or anything. Well, we were young boys, so I mean, we could take anything. But I mean, there were old people there, grandmothers, and babies, and newly-born babies, and sick people. The dead bodies had to-- were thrown into the rabbi's office. There was a rabbi's office, where it was chockablock full with dead bodies, already piled up high-- nobody to bury them or take them away. Anyhow, luckily next morning then they opened the doors. And we marched to the trains. Now that was a very bad day for me, because it was a very hot day. It was already-- it was already June. But you're talking about-- I-- I-- I ent-- I arrived to the ghetto approximately on the 16th of May-- 16th or 17th. And this happened-- when we were marched to the trains, my best guess would-- would have been around-- around the 3rd of June-- 3rd of June, 1944. We marched to the trains. And when we arrived to the station, everything had to be [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Everything has to be done in a terrific hurry. So people had no time to think, and beaten all the time-- beaten with sticks. So quickly into the trains. Well, I had no trouble, being-- being 20 year old, 21 year old. But some old people had to be lifted up and thrown in. And they kept pressing and pushing, pushing more in and more in. And then it was-- everybody was in. Then-- then they wrote a number on the outside of the cattle wagon-- that 87, or 94, or 79, or whatever. The numbers were around from 75 to 100 in each of these wagons. And then, in the middle of each wagon, they-- usually, they put in a-- a bucket for the toilet purposes, one bucket for 96 people. And-- and if you were lucky-- we had actually a second bucket, which contained cold water. I mean, that-- that was gone in the first 15 minutes, the water. And then they locked the doors. There was only a very tiny little ventilation window, maybe 15 by 40 centimeters-- something like that-- and very little air in. And it was a hot, very hot, day. The sun was shining on us in the hot closed-- there was very little oxygen. Extremely hot. I know we all stripped to the waist, took off everything-- shirts. And even then, we had difficulty getting air. The older people passed out. And some of them died. The train was standing there all day, from 9 o'clock in the morning until sunset, in the terrific heat-- no water, nothing, just boiling hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, around that time, around sunset time, this train-- train started to move. And then a bit of air came in. And as we went, the train-- the train went in, right to the ghetto. I mean, it was a special railway track-- industrial track, which was there. And we went through the whole-- whole outer suburbs of the city. And wherever we went, the Hungarians were jumping around. The children had balloons in their hands. And they're shouting that ah, the bloody Jews are going. Wonderful. We have no more Jews. Isn't that a wonderful day?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five days later, the Russians almost wiped out the whole city. They had massive bombing attack. I only learned about this in Auschwitz, where we read in the papers it was a huge bombing attacks against this city. So [LAUGHS] that was-- that was a good-- good news for us, when we arrived to Auschwitz. Anyhow, but at this stage we were in the wagons. And [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] you want to know what happened on the trip, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the biggest problem was, of course, to-- the toilet. The-- the toilet problem was a very big problem. Well, what happened eventually, that one mans-- two men were in charge of the toilet. And each time the ladies had to-- had to go to the bucket, these two men put up a-- one of-- we had our blankets in our rucksacks, so we took out two blankets. And one was holding it in one direction, the other one in the other direction, so the lady could go to the bucket and do what she had to do. The men didn't worry about it, so-- and then, from time to time, we-- we threw the contents out, through this little tiny window. But half of it came back into our face anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as the train started to move, and then it went all-- all night, all day, all night, all day. I-- I lost track of the time, but probably it went for three to four days-- something like that. Then it got cooler. Nighttime it was quite cold, because we went through the high mountains. We went through Slovakia. Could we rest for a minute please, Frank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we stop for a minute, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of the interview with Frank Gipps. It's the 20th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
